gold_final_answer: a_n = n + C for some nonnegative integer C
topics_flat: Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Sequences and Series > Recurrence relations
Algebra > Equations and Inequalities > Linear and quadratic inequalities
Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Greatest common divisors (gcd)
prompt:
Let $p \neq q$ be coprime positive integers. Determine all infinite sequences $a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots$ of positive integers such that the following conditions hold for all $n \geqslant 1$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \max \left(a_{n}, a_{n+1}, \ldots, a_{n+p}\right)-\min \left(a_{n}, a_{n+1}, \ldots, a_{n+p}\right)=p \quad \text{ and } \\
& \max \left(a_{n}, a_{n+1}, \ldots, a_{n+q}\right)-\min \left(a_{n}, a_{n+1}, \ldots, a_{n+q}\right)=q .
\end{aligned}
$$
response_text:
We will show that the only such sequences are arithmetic progressions with common difference \(1\) or \(-1\); since the terms are positive integers for all \(n\ge1\), only the constant-\(+1\) case survives.

## Step 1: Reformulate with successive differences
Let
\[
d_n=a_{n+1}-a_n \qquad (n\ge1).
\]
For any block \(a_n,a_{n+1},\dots,a_{n+r}\), all terms are integers, so if
\[
\max(a_n,\dots,a_{n+r})-\min(a_n,\dots,a_{n+r})=r,
\]
then necessarily all \(r+1\) terms are distinct and fill exactly the \(r+1\) consecutive integers between the minimum and maximum.

Hence, for every \(n\),

- the \(p+1\) numbers \(a_n,\dots,a_{n+p}\) are exactly \(p+1\) consecutive integers;
- the \(q+1\) numbers \(a_n,\dots,a_{n+q}\) are exactly \(q+1\) consecutive integers.

In particular, in each such block, consecutive terms differ by \(\pm1\). So for all \(n\),
\[
d_n\in\{1,-1\}.
\]

## Step 2: A local characterization
Fix \(r\in\{p,q\}\). Since \(a_n,\dots,a_{n+r}\) are \(r+1\) consecutive integers, the walk
\[
0,\ d_n,\ d_n+d_{n+1},\ \dots,\ d_n+\cdots+d_{n+r-1}
\]
must visit \(r+1\) distinct integers lying in an interval of length \(r\). Therefore it must be exactly a monotone walk through consecutive integers. Hence all the involved signs are equal:
\[
d_n=d_{n+1}=\cdots=d_{n+r-1}.
\]
So for every \(n\),
\[
d_n=\cdots=d_{n+p-1} \qquad\text{and}\qquad d_n=\cdots=d_{n+q-1}.
\]

Thus each \(d_n\) equals all the next \(p-1\) and all the next \(q-1\) differences.

## Step 3: Use coprimality
From the above,
\[
d_n=d_{n+p}\quad\text{and}\quad d_n=d_{n+q}
\]
for all \(n\), because the blocks of equal signs overlap:
\[
d_n=\cdots=d_{n+p-1},\qquad d_{n+1}=\cdots=d_{n+p},
\]
so \(d_n=d_{n+p}\), and similarly \(d_n=d_{n+q}\).

Hence \((d_n)\) has period \(p\) and period \(q\). Since \(\gcd(p,q)=1\), it has period \(1\). Therefore \(d_n\) is constant:
\[
d_n\equiv \varepsilon\in\{1,-1\}.
\]

So
\[
a_n=a_1+(n-1)\varepsilon.
\]

## Step 4: Check positivity
If \(\varepsilon=-1\), then \(a_n=a_1-(n-1)\) is eventually nonpositive, impossible for an infinite sequence of positive integers.

Thus \(\varepsilon=1\), and
\[
a_n=a_1+n-1
\]
for some positive integer \(a_1\).

## Step 5: Verify
For such a sequence,
\[
a_n,a_{n+1},\dots,a_{n+r}=a_n,a_n+1,\dots,a_n+r,
\]
so the maximum minus minimum is exactly \(r\), for both \(r=p\) and \(r=q\).

Therefore all solutions are exactly the sequences
\[
a_n=n+c\qquad(n\ge1),
\]
where \(c\ge0\) is an integer.

Final answer: \(\boxed{a_n=a_1+n-1\text{ for all }n\ge1,\ \text{with }a_1\in\mathbb Z_{>0}}\).
